skill.name: skill-creator
skill.description: Creates and validates reusable AI assistant skills with guided planning, templates, installation guidance, and packaging utilities.
skill.summary: Create reusable AI assistant skills with structured planning, templates, validation, and packaging support.
skill.author: Eric Andrade

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, and filesystem patterns, but most command and network hits are markdown examples, license URLs, or template text. AI review found no evidence of malicious network exfiltration or prompt injection, but the shipped scripts and installation guidance can write files, create archives, and create global symlinks, so publication should include a filesystem warning.

medium_findings.title: Path-Controlled Skill Scaffolding Writes Files
medium_findings.description: The initializer combines a user-provided path and skill name, creates the resulting directory, writes SKILL.md, creates resource directories, writes placeholder files, and marks an example script executable. This is expected for a scaffolding tool, but it can modify arbitrary user-writable filesystem locations if arguments are careless or adversarial.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly creates directories and writes files based on command-line arguments. The behavior is legitimate scaffolding, but the filesystem impact is real and not limited to a fixed safe directory.

medium_findings.title: Global Symlink Installation Guidance Can Overwrite Skill Links
medium_findings.description: The documentation recommends forced symlink creation into assistant home directories. This is a legitimate installation pattern, but ln -sf can replace existing symlinks and affects global assistant behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show forced symlink commands and verification of home-directory skill paths. The commands are presented as installation steps, so the risk is contextual rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Archive Packaging Can Include Unintended Files
medium_findings.description: The packager walks every file under the selected skill directory and writes it into a .skill archive after basic validation. This can accidentally include secrets or unrelated files if the source directory is not reviewed first.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The recursive archive creation is explicit and does not filter sensitive filenames. There is no upload path, so the concern is accidental disclosure through later sharing of the archive.

low_findings.title: Static Network Findings Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings occur in the license, clone examples, and references. No executable HTTP client, fetch call, or credential-bearing outbound request was found in the inspected files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is limited to URLs in text and example clone commands. A targeted search found no requests, urllib, curl, wget, socket, fetch, or similar executable network client in the scripts.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Findings Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts appear to be triggered by ordinary words inside license text, documentation, validation messages, and template guidance. No cryptographic API use was found in the reviewed scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected code imports pathlib, zipfile, yaml, re, os, and sys, but not cryptographic libraries. The flagged lines are not cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Were Counted As Execution
low_findings.description: Many external-command findings are shell examples or illustrative markdown blocks. They are not executed by the package scripts, although users should still review commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited command text is contained in markdown documentation. The Python scripts do not call subprocess, os.system, eval, or exec.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Writes From User-Controlled Arguments
dangerous_patterns.description: The script uses command-line path and skill-name inputs to choose where files and directories are created. This pattern requires caller review and path validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path construction and later mkdir call are directly visible. The script documents naming requirements but does not enforce them before using the arguments.

dangerous_patterns.title: Forced Symlink Creation In Home Directories
dangerous_patterns.description: The installation guidance uses ln -sf against assistant skill directories under the user home. This can alter globally loaded skills.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The symlink commands and target directories are explicit. The pattern is legitimate installation behavior but still security-relevant.

content.value_statement: Creating a reusable skill can require many structure, metadata, and validation decisions. This skill guides planning, scaffolds files, validates frontmatter, and prepares skills for local use or packaging.
actual_capabilities: Guides users through defining a skill purpose, trigger conditions, supported platforms, and installation target.
Provides markdown workflow instructions for creating skill directories for Copilot, Claude Code, and Codex.
Includes a Python initializer that creates SKILL.md plus scripts, references, and assets placeholder files.
Includes a Python validator that checks SKILL.md frontmatter and common naming rules.
Includes a Python packager that validates a skill folder and writes a .skill archive.
Documents global symlink installation patterns for supported assistant tools.
limitations: The initializer creates generic placeholder content that still needs human editing.
The validator checks basic structure and does not prove skill quality or security.
Global installation examples can overwrite existing symlinks if used without review.
The scripts rely on local filesystem access and do not manage permissions or backups.

use_cases.title: Create a Team Skill Template
use_cases.description: Generate a consistent starting structure for a team-specific assistant skill.

use_cases.title: Package Domain Knowledge
use_cases.description: Turn recurring procedures, references, and helper scripts into a reusable assistant skill.

use_cases.title: Validate Skill Metadata
use_cases.description: Check SKILL.md frontmatter and naming before sharing a skill with other users.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for creating a simple new skill.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new skill for summarizing support tickets. Ask me only for the details you need, then scaffold the files.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Workflow Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for a skill with sequential steps.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a workflow-based skill for release note preparation. Include trigger phrases, required references, validation steps, and installation options.

prompt_templates.title: Add Bundled Resources
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for scripts, references, and assets.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a skill that includes a helper script, a reference guide, and reusable asset placeholders. Explain what each resource should contain.

prompt_templates.title: Audit and Package a Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for preparing a skill for distribution.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this existing skill for structure, frontmatter, resource placement, and packaging readiness. Fix issues, validate it, and create a distributable archive.

output_examples.input: Create a skill for debugging Python stack traces.
output_examples.output: A planned skill structure with a clear trigger description, SKILL.md outline, optional reference files, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: Validate my skill before I share it.
output_examples.output: A concise validation result that lists frontmatter problems, naming issues, and recommended fixes.
A validated distributable skill archive path plus a summary of included files.

best_practices: Review generated placeholder files before installation or packaging.
Use repository-local installation until the skill has been tested with realistic prompts.
Run validation after editing SKILL.md and before creating a distributable archive.
anti_patterns: Do not package secrets, credentials, or unrelated project files into a skill archive.
Do not use global symlink installation without checking existing skill paths first.
Do not treat generated placeholder guidance as a finished marketplace-ready skill.

faq.question: Does this skill create finished skills automatically?
faq.answer: It creates a structured starting point. A human should still edit instructions, examples, and resources.

faq.question: Which tools does it target?
faq.answer: The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code support, and the instructions mention Copilot paths too.

faq.question: Does it contact external services?
faq.answer: No executable network client was found. The package contains documentation links and clone examples.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: It includes scripts and installation guidance that write files, create archives, and manage global symlinks.

faq.question: Can it validate security quality?
faq.answer: No. The included validator checks basic SKILL.md structure and frontmatter rules only.

faq.question: Should I install generated skills globally immediately?
faq.answer: Test skills locally first, then install globally only after reviewing paths and existing symlinks.
